context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 8


Problem 8.1 (25 pts)

Consider the free transverse oscillations of the two-dimensional beaded string shown in Figure 1. The system is composed of 9 beads arranged in a 3×3 grid. All the horizontal strings have tension Th, all the vertical strings have tension Tv, all the solid circles are beads with mass m. The ends of strings not attached to a bead are fixed. The square frame is fixed in the z = 0 plane.

[Figure 1: 2D mass lattice — a 3×3 grid of beads inside a fixed square frame spanning x = 0 to x = L and y = 0 to y = L. Columns are indexed k = 1, 2, 3 and rows are indexed j = 1, 2, 3.]

(a) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies.
(b) Suppose that Tv = 1000·Th. Draw nine diagrams, one for each normal mode, in order of increasing frequency, indicating which beads are moving up (by a + sign), which are moving down (by a − sign) and which are not moving (by a 0). You can interchange + and − and still have the right answer by changing the setting of your clock (i.e., multiplying your normal mode vector by −1). For example, the lowest frequency mode looks like:
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
while the mode with the fifth highest frequency looks like:
    −  0  +
    0  0  0
    +  0  −


Problem 8.2 (25 pts)

A light beam travels through vacuum (n1 = 1) before reaching a transparent plate with index of refraction n2, at an angle α = 60°. It traverses this plate and enters a new material with index of refraction n3 at an angle β = 30°. The configuration of this optical experiment is shown in Figure 2.

[Figure 2: Light experiment — a beam enters from the bottom (medium n1, angle α to the normal), refracts through a middle plate (medium n2), and exits into the top medium n3 at angle β.]

(a) What is the possible range of values for n2?
(b) What is the value for n3?


Problem 8.3 (25 pts)

Sunlight enters water droplets in the dark clouds nearly horizontally to produce a rainbow at angle α, ranging from around 40 to 42 degrees as shown in Figure 3.

[Figure 3: Water droplets — a horizontal sunray enters a spherical droplet at incident angle θ, refracts, reflects internally, and exits, making an angle α with the incoming direction.]

(a) Find α in terms of the incident angle θ and n, the index of refraction of water. Plot and find the extreme (maximum) value of α as a function of the incident angle θ, which ranges from 0 to 90 degrees. (Use n = 1.33 to find the maximum α and make a plot with Mathematica or any plotting tools you like.)
(b) Why does the rainbow appear at the extreme value of α?
(c) Which color is at higher angle when you look up at the rainbow? (Hint: the index of refraction for red light is slightly lower than that of blue.)
(d) How do you explain the double rainbows? Which color is higher in the sky for the second rainbow? A qualitative discussion is sufficient for this part.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m trying to work on Problem Set 8, but I’m kind of unsure where to start. Could we begin with Problem 8.1 and set up the equations of motion for the 3×3 bead grid?
exchanges.tutor: Good choice to start with 8.1! Before we set up the full 3×3 system, let's think small. Consider just one bead somewhere in the interior of the grid. What forces act on it due to the strings connected to it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting fresh on 8.1. I should help them engage with the problem by asking them to think about what they already know, rather than launching into a full setup. I'll ask a targeted question to get them thinking about the physics of a single bead first, which is the natural starting point for guided discovery.

exchanges.student: I think it gets forces from the four neighboring strings: left, right, up, and down. Since this is transverse motion, I’m guessing each force depends on the difference in the bead’s vertical displacement compared to the neighbor, but I’m not totally sure how the tensions \(T_h\) and \(T_v\) enter.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track! Think back to the 1D beaded string. If a bead is displaced transversely by some amount, and its neighbor is displaced by a different amount, how does the tension in that string segment produce a restoring force on the bead? What's the formula for that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the four neighbors and the transverse displacement idea. They're unsure how Th and Tv enter. I should nudge them to think about the standard transverse string force formula they should know from earlier in the course, without giving it away.

exchanges.student: I think for small displacements it’s something like tension times the slope difference. So for a horizontal neighbor maybe the force is \(T_h\frac{z_{\text{neighbor}}-z}{a}\), where \(a\) is the spacing between beads? Then I’d add left and right contributions, and similarly vertical ones with \(T_v\). Is that the right idea?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now can you write the full equation of motion for a general interior bead at position (j,k), combining all four contributions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the force formula. Now I should push them to write the full equation of motion for a general interior bead (j,k), combining all four contributions. This is the natural next step before moving to normal modes.

exchanges.student:
Let me try: if \(z_{j,k}(t)\) is the transverse displacement, then

\[
m\ddot z_{j,k}
=
\frac{T_h}{a}\big(z_{j,k+1}-z_{j,k}\big)
+
\frac{T_h}{a}\big(z_{j,k-1}-z_{j,k}\big)
+
\frac{T_v}{a}\big(z_{j+1,k}-z_{j,k}\big)
+
\frac{T_v}{a}\big(z_{j-1,k}-z_{j,k}\big).
\]

So I think that simplifies to

\[
m\ddot z_{j,k}
=
\frac{T_h}{a}\big(z_{j,k+1}+z_{j,k-1}-2z_{j,k}\big)
+
\frac{T_v}{a}\big(z_{j+1,k}+z_{j-1,k}-2z_{j,k}\big).
\]

I’m assuming \(a=L/4\) since there are 3 interior beads between fixed ends.
exchanges.tutor: That's correct, well done. Now, to find the normal modes, you need to propose an ansatz for z_{j,k}(t). Given the boundary conditions (fixed ends) and the separable structure of your equation, what form would you guess for z_{j,k}(t)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly written the equation of motion. The spacing assumption a=L/4 is correct. Now I should guide them toward the normal mode ansatz — this is the key next step to find the normal modes and frequencies.

exchanges.student:
I’d guess a separable standing-wave form, maybe

\[
z_{j,k}(t)=A_{j,k}\cos(\omega t+\phi),
\]

and then for the spatial part something like

\[
A_{j,k}\propto \sin\!\left(\frac{p\pi j}{4}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{q\pi k}{4}\right)
\]

with \(p,q=1,2,3\), because the boundaries are fixed at \(j,k=0,4\). I’m a little unsure if I assigned \(j\) to vertical and \(k\) to horizontal the same way as the problem does.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right — the index convention doesn't change the physics here. Now substitute your ansatz into the equation of motion. What do you get for ω in terms of p, q, Th, Tv, m, and a?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the separable sine ansatz and the correct range of p and q. The index assignment concern is minor and doesn't affect the physics. I should affirm this and push them to substitute into the equation of motion to find the dispersion relation (the frequencies), which is the key remaining step for part (a).

exchanges.student:
Let me try the substitution. I think it gives

\[
m(-\omega^2)A_{j,k}
=
\frac{T_h}{a}\big[A_{j,k+1}+A_{j,k-1}-2A_{j,k}\big]
+
\frac{T_v}{a}\big[A_{j+1,k}+A_{j-1,k}-2A_{j,k}\big].
\]

Using the sine form, I think each bracket turns into a factor like \(-4\sin^2(\cdots/2)\), so maybe

\[
\omega_{pq}^2=\frac{4}{ma}\left[T_h\sin^2\!\left(\frac{q\pi}{8}\right)+T_v\sin^2\!\left(\frac{p\pi}{8}\right)\right].
\]

I might have swapped \(p\) and \(q\), but I think that’s the idea.
exchanges.tutor: That's correct — well done on part (a)! Now for part (b), you have 9 modes corresponding to (p,q) with p,q ∈ {1,2,3}. Given that Tv = 1000·Th, which term in your ω² expression will dominate for most modes? How does that help you order the modes by frequency?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the dispersion relation. This completes part (a). I should confirm this and move them toward part (b), where they need to order the 9 modes by frequency and draw the displacement diagrams. The key challenge there is using Tv = 1000*Th to figure out the ordering.

exchanges.student:
I think the \(T_v\) term dominates almost completely, so the frequency is mostly set by \(p\), not \(q\). So first I’d group modes by \(p=1,2,3\), and within each group the \(q\)-dependence from \(T_h\) just gives small splittings. 

So I’m guessing all the \(p=1\) modes are the lowest three, then all the \(p=2\) modes, then all the \(p=3\) modes. Can I check next how the pattern of signs comes from the \(\sin\!\left(\frac{p\pi j}{4}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{q\pi k}{4}\right)\) factors?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on the grouping. Now just evaluate your sine factors directly. For example, for p=1, q=1, compute sin(pπj/4) for j=1,2,3 and sin(qπk/4) for k=1,2,3. What signs do you get for each bead?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the grouping by p. Now they want to work out the sign patterns. I should encourage them to do this themselves by evaluating the sine factors for each (p,q) combination rather than explaining it to them.

exchanges.student:
For \(p=1\), I get \(\sin(\pi/4), \sin(\pi/2), \sin(3\pi/4)\), so that’s all positive. And for \(q=1\) it’s the same across the columns, so I think every bead is \(+\), which matches the example.

I think I get that one, but I’m a little shaky on how to do \(q=2\) and \(q=3\) quickly without messing up the signs.
exchanges.tutor: Good. Just do the same thing for q=2: compute sin(2πk/4) for k=1,2,3. What values do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got p=1,q=1 correctly and is asking for help with q=2 and q=3. I should prompt them to just evaluate the sine values numerically for q=2 rather than explaining it, keeping them doing the work.

exchanges.student: I get \(\sin(\pi/2)=1\), \(\sin(\pi)=0\), and \(\sin(3\pi/2)=-1\). So the column pattern should be \(+\;0\;-\), and since \(p=1\) makes all the rows positive, I think the whole mode is just that same pattern repeated in each row.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — that's the (p=1, q=2) mode. You clearly have the method down. Try working out all 9 sign diagrams on your own now, remembering to order them by increasing frequency (grouping by p first, then ordering within each group by q). Write them out and we can review.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked out the (p=1, q=2) mode. They now have the tools to complete all 9 modes on their own. Given we've been working through the mechanics for a while, I should encourage them to now attempt all 9 diagrams themselves rather than continuing step by step.

exchanges.student:
Okay, I think the row factors are:

- \(p=1:\; (+,+,+)\)
- \(p=2:\; (+,0,-)\)
- \(p=3:\; (+,-,+)\)

and the column factors are the same pattern for \(q=1,2,3\). So each mode is the outer product of the row-sign pattern and column-sign pattern.

Can I paste my 9 grids next and have you check the ordering too?
exchanges.tutor: Your row and column factors are all correct. Yes, go ahead and paste your 9 grids with their (p,q) labels and ordering — I'll take a look.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified all row and column factors and understands the outer product structure. They're ready to write out all 9 grids. I should affirm and invite them to share their work.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic pedagogy throughout the conversation. Every key reasoning step was elicited from the student rather than given directly: the tutor asked about forces on a single bead, prompted the student to recall the 1D formula, asked for the full equation of motion, prompted the ansatz, asked for the substitution result, and then guided the sign-diagram work step by step. The student did the intellectual heavy lifting at each stage. Responses were concise and well-targeted, tone was supportive, and the conversation progressed logically without redundancy or drift.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a final answer or near-submission-ready output. All key ideas (force formula, equation of motion, ansatz, frequency expression, sign patterns) were derived by the student with targeted prompting. No 1.1.A, 1.1.B, or 1.1.C violations are present. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student progress before moving forward and asked focused diagnostic questions rather than dumping information. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., confirming the method of evaluating sine factors, noting the outer-product structure) rather than only correctness judgments. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetition without progress, no off-topic drift, and no misunderstanding of the assignment went uncorrected. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were short and interactive, language was clear, tone was warm and encouraging, and no grading language was used.
